1: Ps 108: Composite Psalm — Confidence in YHWH's Faithfulness for Battle. Ps 108 is a liturgical compilation that combines Ps 57:7-11 (= Ps 108:1-5) and Ps 60:5-12 (= Ps 108:6-13). The phenomenon of combining portions of earlier psalms into a new composition is attested in the Psalter (cf. Ps 135 drawing from multiple sources). The new context changes the meaning: taken from their individual lament settings and combined, the doxological section (vv1-5) is placed in service of a national military petition (vv6-13). 'My heart is steadfast, O God! I will sing and make melody with all my being!' (nacon libbi Elohim, ashirah va'azamerah af kevodi) — the firm resolve of the worshiper. 'Glory' (kavod) as the organ of praise — the psalmist's weightiest, most dignified capacity will praise.
2: 'Awake, O harp and lyre! I will awake the dawn!' — urah hanevel vekinor (awake, O lute and harp!) — the musical instruments addressed as if sleeping, summoned to wakefulness for early morning worship. 'I will awake the dawn' (a'irah shachar) — the psalmist rises before dawn to begin praise: the paradox of waking the dawn by beginning worship before it arrives. This early morning devotion is also in Ps 5:3; 59:16; 63:1.
3: 'I will give thanks to you, O LORD, among the peoples; I will sing praises to you among the nations' — the universal audience of the praise: 'among the peoples' (vaadim) and 'among the nations' (bale'umim). The individual's thanksgiving reaches beyond Israel to all peoples — an anticipation of the universal praise of Ps 117 and the NT mission's rationale (Rom 15:9-11 cites such Psalms to establish the Gentile mission).
4: 'For your steadfast love is great above the heavens; your faithfulness reaches to the clouds' — 'great above the heavens' (gadol meal shamayim chasdecha) — the chesed of YHWH exceeds even the immensity of the heavens. 'Faithfulness reaches to the clouds' (vead shechaqim amitecha) — emet (faithfulness/truth) measured in atmospheric distance. The two covenant qualities (chesed and emet) that appeared together in Ps 85:10 (where they 'meet') are here measured cosmologically: they surpass and fill all created space.
5: 'Be exalted, O God, above the heavens! Let your glory be over all the earth!' — the doxological petition: 'be exalted above the heavens' (rumah al hashamayim Elohim) — even the measure of v4 (your chesed is above the heavens) leads to the petition that YHWH himself be exalted above his own attributes' measure. 'Your glory over all the earth' (veal kol haaretz kevodecha) — the universal scope of the divine glory-claim.
6: 'That your beloved ones may be rescued; give salvation by your right hand and answer us!' — the transition from doxology to petition. 'Your beloved ones' (yedidecha) — the covenant-beloved, Israel. The 'right hand' (yeminecha) — YHWH's power-hand, the instrument of military rescue. The petition grounds the rescue request in the doxology: the God whose chesed exceeds the heavens is the one being asked to apply that chesed to the national crisis.
7: 'God has promised in his holiness: I will exult, I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Valley of Succoth' — the divine oracle (from Ps 60:6-8). The specific geography: Shechem (central highlands, the ancient covenant-assembly site: Gen 33:18-20; Josh 24) and the Valley of Succoth (Transjordan, east of the Jordan). YHWH's claim over these territories — divided between the tribal allotments — is the basis of the petition. The land belongs to YHWH and was allocated by him.
8: 'Gilead is mine; Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter' — the tribal catalog: Gilead (Transjordan territory of the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh), Manasseh (half in Transjordan, half in Cisjordan), Ephraim ('my helmet' — the major military tribe of the north; rosh Ephraim, the head/foremost of the northern tribes), Judah ('my scepter' — the royal tribe, the seat of Davidic authority). YHWH speaks of the tribes as his possessions and instruments.
9: 'Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I cast my shoe; over Philistia I shout in triumph' — the catalog continues with subjugated nations: Moab as 'washbasin' (sir rachtziy) — the basin into which dirty water is thrown, a symbol of contemptible servitude. Edom: YHWH 'casts his shoe' (al-Edom ashlikh na'ali) — claiming ownership (Ruth 4:7: removing or throwing a sandal was a property-transfer symbol). Philistia: 'I shout in triumph' (hitroha'i, Hithpolel of rua). All three traditional enemies are subjected to YHWH's authority.
10: 'Who will bring me into the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom?' — the psalmist's question arising from the oracle: if YHWH has declared ownership of all these territories (vv7-9), why is Edom's 'fortified city' (ir matzor, probably Petra/Sela, the rock-fortress of Edom) still beyond reach? The gap between the divine declaration of ownership and the human military reality is the psalm's tension. Knowing YHWH owns Edom does not automatically open the campaign route.
11: 'Have you not rejected us, O God? You do not go out, O God, with our armies' — the lament beneath the confidence: 'have you not rejected us?' (halo attah Elohim zenavtanu) — the complaint that YHWH's presence with the army has been withdrawn. The military theology of Israel: YHWH goes out with Israel's armies as the divine warrior (Num 10:35: 'Arise, O LORD, and let your enemies be scattered'; 1 Sam 4:3-11 — the tragedy when the ark's presence does not guarantee victory). The oracle (vv7-9) was the declaration of divine ownership; the lament (v11) is the gap in actual experience.
12: 'Oh grant us help against the foe, for vain is the salvation of man!' — 'vain is the salvation of man' (shav teshu'at adam) — the key theological principle of Ps 108: human military power alone accomplishes nothing. The only efficacious help (ezrat) must come from YHWH. This is the military theology of Ps 20:7 ('some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God').
13: 'With God we shall do valiantly; it is he who will tread down our foes' — the psalm's conclusion in confident faith: be-Elohim na'aseh chayil (with God we will do powerfully/valiantly). The victory is neither automatic (the oracle does not guarantee military ease) nor human (the lament of v11 shows the limits of human effort) but covenantal: 'with God' the enemies are trodden down. The final word is YHWH's agency, not Israel's.
